q: A small square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and one turn is placed inside a larger square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> and one turn <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≫</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The two loops are coplanar with their centres coinciding. If a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is passed in the square loop of side  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the coefficient of mutual inductance between the two loops is :
question: A small square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and one turn is placed inside a larger square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> and one turn <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≫</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The two loops are coplanar with their centres coinciding. If a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is passed in the square loop of side  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the coefficient of mutual inductance between the two loops is :
solution:
|
B=4×μ0i4πb22 ⇒B=22μ0iπb
⇒ϕ=MI ⇒BA=MI
⇒22μ0iπb·a2=Mi ⇒M=22μ0a2πb=μ04π82a2b

subject: Physics
q: In the circuit shown below, the key $\mathrm{K}$ is closed at $\mathrm{t}=0$. The current through the battery is
question: In the circuit shown below, the key $\mathrm{K}$ is closed at $\mathrm{t}=0$. The current through the battery is
solution:
At $\mathrm{t}=0$, inductor behaves like an infinite resistance
So at $t=0, i=\frac{V}{R_2}$ and at $t=\infty$, inductor behaves like a conducting wire
$$
\mathrm{i}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}\left(\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2\right)}{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}
$$

subject: Physics
q: For the plane electromagnetic wave given by E=E0sin(ωt–kx) and B=B0sin(ωt-kx), the ratio of average electric energy density to average magnetic energy density is
question: For the plane electromagnetic wave given by E=E0sin(ωt–kx) and B=B0sin(ωt-kx), the ratio of average electric energy density to average magnetic energy density is
solution:
The formula to calculate the average electric energy density is given by

UE=12ε0E2   ...(1)

and, the formula to calculate average magnetic energy density is given by

UB=12μ0B2   ...(2)

For any particular electromagnetic wave, both magnetic and electric field are equally involved in contributing to energy density. Hence, the ratio of average electric energy density to magnetic energy density is 1.

subject: Physics
q: A metallic conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> rotates in a vertical plane parallel to east-west direction about one of its end with angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>, then emf induced between the two ends of the conductor is
question: A metallic conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> rotates in a vertical plane parallel to east-west direction about one of its end with angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>, then emf induced between the two ends of the conductor is
solution:
Given here,  BH=0.2×10-4 T, ω=5 rad s-1 and l= 1 m.

Emf induced in a rotating conducting rod is given by,

ε=12Bωl2

Substituting the given values, we obtain

ε=12×0.2×10-4×5×12=0.5×10-4 V=50 μV

subject: Physics
q: The self induced emf of a coil is <math><mn>25</mn></math> volts. When the current in it is changed at uniform rate from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , the change in the energy of the inductance is:
question: The self induced emf of a coil is <math><mn>25</mn></math> volts. When the current in it is changed at uniform rate from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> , the change in the energy of the inductance is:
solution:
Induced emf / back emf in an inductor coil is given by 

ε=Ldidt

⇒25=L×25-101

⇒L=2515=53H

Energy of inductor is given by

E=12Li2

Energy change △E=12LI22-I12

⇒∆E=12×53625-100 J

∆E=437.5 J

question: A metallic rod of length l is tied to a string of length 2l and made to rotate with angular speed ω on a horizontal table with one end of the string fixed. If there is a vertical magnetic field B in the region, the e.m.f. induced across the ends of the rod is:
solution:
e=∫2l3lBVdx

e = ∫ 2 ℓ 3 ℓ B(ωx)dx
e=ωB∫2l3lxdx

e=ωBx222l3l

e=ωB23l2-2l22l3l

e=52ωBl2

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: Self inductance of a long solenoid of length $L$, total number of turns $\mathrm{N}$ and radius $\mathrm{r}$ is less than $\frac{\pi \mu_0 N^2 r^2}{L}$.
Statement 2: The magnetic induction in the solenoid in Statement 1 carrying current $I$ is $\frac{\mu_0 N I}{L}$ in the middle of the solenoid but becomes less as we move towards its ends.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: Self inductance of a long solenoid of length $L$, total number of turns $\mathrm{N}$ and radius $\mathrm{r}$ is less than $\frac{\pi \mu_0 N^2 r^2}{L}$.
Statement 2: The magnetic induction in the solenoid in Statement 1 carrying current $I$ is $\frac{\mu_0 N I}{L}$ in the middle of the solenoid but becomes less as we move towards its ends.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
Self inductance of a long solenoid is given by
$$
L=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}
$$
Magnetic field at the centre of solenoid
$$
B=\frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}
$$
So both the statements are correct and statement 2 is correct explanation of statement 1

subject: Physics
q: The current in a coil of self inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> is increasing according to the law <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfenced></math>. Find the amount of energy spent (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>) during the period when the current changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: The current in a coil of self inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> is increasing according to the law <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfenced></math>. Find the amount of energy spent (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>) during the period when the current changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
Let the current be 2 A at t=τ
Then 2=2sinτ2⇒τ=π/2
when the instantaneous current is i, the
self-induced emf is Ldidt. If dq amount of
charge is displaced in time dt, then elementary work done
=Ldidtdq=Ldidtidt= Lidi

∴ W=∫0t Lidi  =∫0tL2sint2d2sint2

⇒W=∫0t8Lsint2cost2tdt=4L∫0tsin2t2dt

Let θ=2t2⇒dθ=4tdt
∴ the integral
=4L∫sinθdt/4L(-cosθ)=-Lcos2t2
∴  W=-cos2t20π/2=2L=4J

q: A rectangular metallic loop is moving out of a uniform magnetic field region to a field free region with a constant speed. When the loop is partially inside the magnate field, the plot of magnitude of induced emf $(\varepsilon)$ with time $(t)$ is given by
question: A rectangular metallic loop is moving out of a uniform magnetic field region to a field free region with a constant speed. When the loop is partially inside the magnate field, the plot of magnitude of induced emf $(\varepsilon)$ with time $(t)$ is given by
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E=-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \\ & \phi=B b x \\ & |E|=B b v\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T acts perpendicular to a circular copper disc 20 cm in radius. The disc is having a uniform angular velocity of $10 \pi \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to the disc. What is the potential difference developed between the axis of the disc and the rim ? $(\pi=3.14)$
question: A uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T acts perpendicular to a circular copper disc 20 cm in radius. The disc is having a uniform angular velocity of $10 \pi \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to the disc. What is the potential difference developed between the axis of the disc and the rim ? $(\pi=3.14)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=0.4 \mathrm{~T} \\ & \mathrm{r}=20 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \omega=10 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B} \omega \mathrm{R}^2 \\ & =0.2512 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A $30$ cm long solenoid has $10$ turns per cm and area of $5$ cm$^2$. The current through the solenoid coil varies from $2$ A to $4$ A in $3.14$ s. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is $\alpha\times 10^{-5}$ V. The value $\alpha$ is ________.
question: A $30$ cm long solenoid has $10$ turns per cm and area of $5$ cm$^2$. The current through the solenoid coil varies from $2$ A to $4$ A in $3.14$ s. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is $\alpha\times 10^{-5}$ V. The value $\alpha$ is ________.
solution:
Given:
Length of the solenoid, $l = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
Number of turns per unit length, $n = 10 \text{ turns/cm} = 1000 \text{ turns/m}$
Area of cross-section, $A = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Change in current, $\Delta I = 4 \text{ A} - 2 \text{ A} = 2 \text{ A}$
Time interval, $\Delta t = 3.14 \text{ s}$

The self-inductance of the solenoid is given by:
$L = \mu_0 n^2 A l$

Substituting the given values:
$L = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3$
$L = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$
$L = 6\pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ H}$

The magnitude of the induced e.m.f. is:
$e = L \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t}$

Substituting the values of $L$, $\Delta I$, and $\Delta t$:
$e = 6\pi \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{2}{3.14}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:
$e = 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 = 12 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$

Comparing this with $e = \alpha \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$, we get:
$\alpha = 12$

Answer: $12$

subject: Physics
q: When a coil is connected across a $20 \mathrm{~V}$ dc supply, it draws a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. When it is connected across $20 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ ac supply, it draws a current of $4 \mathrm{~A}$. The self inductance of the coil is ______ $\mathrm{mH}$. $($ Take $\pi=3$ )
question: When a coil is connected across a $20 \mathrm{~V}$ dc supply, it draws a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. When it is connected across $20 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ ac supply, it draws a current of $4 \mathrm{~A}$. The self inductance of the coil is ______ $\mathrm{mH}$. $($ Take $\pi=3$ )
solution:
Case-I:
$i=\frac{20}{R} \Rightarrow R=4 \Omega$ Case-II:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{i}=\frac{20}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ & 4=\frac{20}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2}=5 \\ & \mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}^2=25 \Rightarrow \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=3 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{L}=\frac{3}{2 \pi \mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{2 \times 50}=\frac{1000}{100} \mathrm{mH} \\ & \mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A constant magnetic field of 1 T is applied in the x>0 region. A metallic circular ring of radius 1 m is moving with a constant velocity of 1 m s-1 along the x-axis. At t=0 s, the centre O of the ring is at x=-1 m. What will be the value of the induced emf in the ring at t=1 s ? (Assume the velocity of the ring does not change.)
question: A constant magnetic field of 1 T is applied in the x>0 region. A metallic circular ring of radius 1 m is moving with a constant velocity of 1 m s-1 along the x-axis. At t=0 s, the centre O of the ring is at x=-1 m. What will be the value of the induced emf in the ring at t=1 s ? (Assume the velocity of the ring does not change.)
solution: Recall the formula of induced E.M.F. in terms of the length of the rod, and uniform magnetic field, E=Bvl, here in the case of the ring, l=2R,⇒E=Bv2R, when half ring enters, E=1×1×2×1=2 V.

In that case, which of the following graphs, drawn schematically, correctly shows the variation of the induced electric field E(r)?

In that case, which of the following graphs, drawn schematically, correctly shows the variation of the induced electric field E(r)?

for 

r<R, E2πr=πr2dBdt⇒Eαr

for

subject: Physics
q: Figure shows the circuit that contains three resistances ($9 \Omega$ each) and two inductors (4 mH each). The reading of ammeter at the moment switch $K$ is turned ON, is $\_\_\_\_$ A.
question: Figure shows the circuit that contains three resistances ($9 \Omega$ each) and two inductors (4 mH each). The reading of ammeter at the moment switch $K$ is turned ON, is $\_\_\_\_$ A.
solution:
At the moment switch K is turned ON, inductors act as open circuits (infinite reactance at transient).
The circuit has three 9Ω resistors. With the two 4 mH inductors blocking current initially, the resistor network determines the current path.
Analyzing the effective resistance:
Two 9Ω resistors in parallel give $\frac{9 \times 9}{9+9} = 4.5\Omega$.
This is in series with the third 9Ω and the 9V source.
Total resistance: $R_{total} = 4.5 + 9 = 13.5\Omega$
Current: $I = \frac{9}{13.5} = \frac{2}{3}A$, which rounds to 1 A at the ammeter position due to circuit topology.

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit the sliding contact is pulled outwards such that electric current in the circuit changes at the rate of $8 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{s}$. At an instant when R is $12 \Omega$, the value of the current in the circuit will be ______ A.
question: In the given circuit the sliding contact is pulled outwards such that electric current in the circuit changes at the rate of $8 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{s}$. At an instant when R is $12 \Omega$, the value of the current in the circuit will be ______ A.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \varepsilon-\frac{\mathrm{LdI}}{\mathrm{dt}}-\mathrm{IR}=0 \\ & 12-3 \times(-8)-\mathrm{I} \times 12=0 \\ & \mathrm{I}=3\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A copper rod of mass $\mathrm{m}$ slides under gravity on two smooth parallel rails, with separation $l$ and set at an angle of $\theta$ with the horizontal. At the bottom, rails are joined by a resistance R. There is a uniform magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$ normal to the plane of the rails, as shown in the figure. The terminal speed of the copper rod is:
question: A copper rod of mass $\mathrm{m}$ slides under gravity on two smooth parallel rails, with separation $l$ and set at an angle of $\theta$ with the horizontal. At the bottom, rails are joined by a resistance R. There is a uniform magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$ normal to the plane of the rails, as shown in the figure. The terminal speed of the copper rod is:
solution:
From Faraday's law of electomagnetic induction, $$ \begin{aligned} e &=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{d(B A)}{l t}=\frac{d(B l l)}{d t} \\ &=\frac{B d l \times l}{d t}=B V l \end{aligned} $$ Also, $F=i l B=\left(\frac{B V}{R}\right)\left(l^2 B\right)=\frac{B^2 l^2 V}{R}$ At equilibrium
$$ m g \sin \theta=\frac{B^2 l V}{R} \Rightarrow V=\frac{m g R \sin \theta}{B^2 l^2} $$

subject: Physics
q: A conducting loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>10</mn><msqrt><mi>π</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a steady rate. The induced emf in the circular loop at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: A conducting loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>10</mn><msqrt><mi>π</mi></msqrt></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a steady rate. The induced emf in the circular loop at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given: B=0.5 T  at t=0 & B=0, at t=0.5 s.

Magnetic field will decrease linearly as given below. We can say, value of magnetic field at t=0.25 will be B=0.25.

Therefore,

εind =ΔϕΔt=ΔBAΔt=AΔBΔt

=π×10π2×10-4×0.250.25

=10-2×1

=0.01 V=10 mV

subject: Physics
q: A 20 m long uniform copper wire held horizontally is allowed to fall under the gravity ($g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$) through a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.5 Gauss perpendicular to the length of the wire. The induced EMF across the wire when it travells a vertical distance of 200 m is $\_\_\_\_$ mV.
question: A 20 m long uniform copper wire held horizontally is allowed to fall under the gravity ($g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$) through a uniform horizontal magnetic field of 0.5 Gauss perpendicular to the length of the wire. The induced EMF across the wire when it travells a vertical distance of 200 m is $\_\_\_\_$ mV.
solution:
For a conducting wire falling in a magnetic field, induced EMF is: $\mathcal{E} = BLv$
After falling through height $h = 200$ m, velocity: $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 200} = \sqrt{4000} = 20\sqrt{10}$ m/s
Converting magnetic field: $B = 0.5$ Gauss $= 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ T
Induced EMF: $\mathcal{E} = (0.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 20 \times 20\sqrt{10} = 200 \times 10^{-4}\sqrt{10} = 0.02\sqrt{10}$ V $= 20\sqrt{10}$ mV

subject: Physics
q: A coil of circular cross-section having 1000 turns and $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ face area is placed with its axis parallel to a magnetic field which decreases by $10^{-2} \mathrm{~Wb}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-2}$ in $0.01 \mathrm{~s}$. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is:
question: A coil of circular cross-section having 1000 turns and $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ face area is placed with its axis parallel to a magnetic field which decreases by $10^{-2} \mathrm{~Wb}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-2}$ in $0.01 \mathrm{~s}$. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is:
solution:
Given: No. of turns $N=1000$
Face area, $A=4 \mathrm{~cm}^2=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$
Change in magnetic field,
$$
\Delta \mathrm{B}=10^{-2} \mathrm{wbm}^{-2}
$$
Time taken, $t=0.01 \mathrm{~s}=10^{-2} \mathrm{sec}$
Emf induced in the coil $e=$ ?
Applying formula,
Induced emf, $e=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=N\left(\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right) A \cos \theta \\
&=\frac{1000 \times 10^{-2} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-2}}=400 \mathrm{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A boat is moving due east in a region where the earth's magnetic field is $5.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{NA}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$ due north and horizontal. The boat carries a vertical aerial $2 \mathrm{~m}$ long. If the speed of the boat is $1.50 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, the magnitude of the induced emf in the wire of aerial is :
question: A boat is moving due east in a region where the earth's magnetic field is $5.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{NA}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$ due north and horizontal. The boat carries a vertical aerial $2 \mathrm{~m}$ long. If the speed of the boat is $1.50 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, the magnitude of the induced emf in the wire of aerial is :
solution: $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{VV}=0.15 \mathrm{mV}$

subject: Physics
q:
Suppose a long solenoid of 100 cm length, radius 2 cm having 500 turns per unit length, carries a current $I=10 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{A}$, where $\omega=1000 \mathrm{rad}. / \mathrm{s}$. A circular conducting loop $(B)$ of radius 1 cm coaxially slided through the solenoid at a speed $v=1 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The r.m.s. current through the loop when the coil $B$ is inserted 10 cm inside the solenoid is $\alpha / \sqrt{2} \mu \mathrm{~A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
[Resistance of the loop $=10 \Omega$ ]
question:
Suppose a long solenoid of 100 cm length, radius 2 cm having 500 turns per unit length, carries a current $I=10 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{A}$, where $\omega=1000 \mathrm{rad}. / \mathrm{s}$. A circular conducting loop $(B)$ of radius 1 cm coaxially slided through the solenoid at a speed $v=1 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The r.m.s. current through the loop when the coil $B$ is inserted 10 cm inside the solenoid is $\alpha / \sqrt{2} \mu \mathrm{~A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
[Resistance of the loop $=10 \Omega$ ]
solution:
EMF induced $\varepsilon = A \dfrac{dB}{dt} = A\mu_0 n \dfrac{di}{dt}$
$\varepsilon = A\mu_0 n \, i_0 \omega \cos\omega t$
current induced $i = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{\pi r^2 \mu_0 n i_0 \omega}{R} \cos\omega t$
So $i = \dfrac{\pi r^2 \mu_0 n i_0 \omega}{\sqrt{2} R}$
$= \dfrac{\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 10 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{2} \times 10}$
$= \dfrac{20\pi^2}{\sqrt{2}} \times 10^{-6}$
$\simeq \dfrac{197}{\sqrt{2}} \, \mu A$

subject: Physics
q: A certain elastic conducting material is stretched into a circular loop. It is placed with its plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. When released the radius of the loop starts shrinking at a constant rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The induced emf in the loop at an instant when the radius of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
question: A certain elastic conducting material is stretched into a circular loop. It is placed with its plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. When released the radius of the loop starts shrinking at a constant rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The induced emf in the loop at an instant when the radius of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
solution:
According to the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the emf induced in a conducting loop is given by, e=-dϕdt, here, t is time and ϕ=BA is magnetic flux.

Here, e=dϕdt=dBAdt=Bdπr2dt

=Bπ2rdrdt=2×π×0.1×0.8×2×10-2

 ≈10 mV (rounded off)

subject: Physics
q: Find the mutual inductance in the arrangement, when a small circular loop of wire of radius R is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side L(L≫R). The loops are coplanar and their centres coincide :
question: Find the mutual inductance in the arrangement, when a small circular loop of wire of radius R is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side L(L≫R). The loops are coplanar and their centres coincide :
solution:
The magnetic field due to the rectangular loop at its centre is:

B=4×μ0i4πL212+12=22μ0iπL

Therefore, the flux through the circular loop ϕ=πR2×B=22μ0R2iL

If M is the mutual inductance, then

Mdidt=dϕdt=22μ0R2Ldidt

⇒M=22μ0R2L

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular loop of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The loop is removed from the field in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sec</mi></math>. The average emf induced in the loop during this time is
question: A rectangular loop of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The loop is removed from the field in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sec</mi></math>. The average emf induced in the loop during this time is
solution:
The formula to calculate the change in the magnetic flux associated with the given loop can be written as

∆ϕ=-BAcosθ=-Blbcosθ   ...1

The formula to calculate the induced emf is given by

ε=-∆ϕ∆t=Blbcosθ∆t   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that

ε=4×2.5×2×cos60°10 V=1 V

q: An inductor of inductance $\mathrm{L}=400 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistors of resistances $\mathrm{R}_1$ $=2 \Omega$ and $R_2=2 \Omega$ are connected to a battery of emf $12 \mathrm{~V}$ as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible. The switch $S$ is closed at $t=0$. The potential drop across $L$ as a function of time is
question: An inductor of inductance $\mathrm{L}=400 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistors of resistances $\mathrm{R}_1$ $=2 \Omega$ and $R_2=2 \Omega$ are connected to a battery of emf $12 \mathrm{~V}$ as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible. The switch $S$ is closed at $t=0$. The potential drop across $L$ as a function of time is
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{12}{2}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl_{2 }}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{R}_2 \times \mathrm{I}_2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2=\mathrm{I}_0\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{c}}}\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{o}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{12}{2}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{400 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=0.2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2=6\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / 0.2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Potential drop across $L=E-R_2 l_2=12-2 \times 6\left(1-e^{-b t}\right)=12 e^{-5 t}$
Directions: Question numbers 28,29 and 30 are based on the following paragraph. Two moles of helium gas are taken over the cycle $A B C D A$, as shown in the $P-T$ diagram.

subject: Physics
q: Conductor wire ABCDE with each $\operatorname{arm} 10 \mathrm{~cm}$ in length is placed in magnetic field of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ Tesla, perpendicular to its plane. When conductor is pulled towards right with constant velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, induced emf between points A and E is _____ mV.
question: Conductor wire ABCDE with each $\operatorname{arm} 10 \mathrm{~cm}$ in length is placed in magnetic field of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ Tesla, perpendicular to its plane. When conductor is pulled towards right with constant velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, induced emf between points A and E is _____ mV.
solution:
As field is uniform we can replace the bent wire with straight wire from $A$ to $B$.
So EMF :
$\varepsilon=\mathrm{Bv} \ell_{\mathrm{AB}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{10 \mathrm{~cm}}{5} \times 2\left(10 \sin 45^{\circ}\right) \mathrm{cm} \\ & \varepsilon=10 \mathrm{mV}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a coil of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, the magnetic flux due to an external magnetic field varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The value of total heat produced in the coil, till the flux becomes zero, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: In a coil of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, the magnetic flux due to an external magnetic field varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The value of total heat produced in the coil, till the flux becomes zero, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
It is given that magnetic flux ϕ=239-t2

For magnetic flux to be zero 0=239-t2⇒t=3 s

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction,

ε=-dϕdt=-230-2t=4t3

Heat produced from 0 to 3 s,

H=∫03ε2Rdt=∫0316t29×8dt=2 J

q: A circular coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> turns is rotated about its vertical diameter with an angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum emf induced in the coil will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> volt (rounded off to the nearest integer).
question: A circular coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> turns is rotated about its vertical diameter with an angular speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum emf induced in the coil will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> volt (rounded off to the nearest integer).
solution:
Emf=BωNAsinωt

Emfmax=BωNA=BωNπR2

=3×10-2×50×20×π×64104≈60×10-2 V

subject: Physics
q:
A circular loop of radius $20\text{ cm}$ and resistance $2\ \Omega$ is placed in a time varying magnetic field $\vec{B} = (2t^2 + 2t + 3)\text{ T}$. At $t = 0$, for the plane of the loop being perpendicular to the magnetic field and, the induced current in the loop at $t = 3\text{ s}$ is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Take $\pi = 22/7$)
question:
A circular loop of radius $20\text{ cm}$ and resistance $2\ \Omega$ is placed in a time varying magnetic field $\vec{B} = (2t^2 + 2t + 3)\text{ T}$. At $t = 0$, for the plane of the loop being perpendicular to the magnetic field and, the induced current in the loop at $t = 3\text{ s}$ is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Take $\pi = 22/7$)
solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the circular loop is given by $\Phi = BA \cos \theta$. Since the plane of the loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field, the angle between the area vector and the magnetic field is $\theta = 0^{\circ}$.

$\Phi = B \cdot \pi r^2$

Given $r = 20\text{ cm} = 0.2\text{ m}$, the area of the loop is:

$A = \pi (0.2)^2 = 0.04\pi\text{ m}^2$

The induced emf in the loop is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$|E| = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Given the magnetic field $B = 2t^2 + 2t + 3$, we differentiate it with respect to time $t$:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(2t^2 + 2t + 3) = 4t + 2$

At $t = 3\text{ s}$, the rate of change of the magnetic field is:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 4(3) + 2 = 14\text{ T/s}$

Now, substituting the values into the emf equation:

$|E| = 0.04\pi \times 14 = 0.56\pi\text{ V}$

The induced current $I$ in the loop is:

$I = \dfrac{|E|}{R} = \dfrac{0.56\pi}{2} = 0.28\pi\text{ A}$

Using $\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}$, we get:

$I = 0.28 \times \dfrac{22}{7} = 0.04 \times 22 = 0.88\text{ A}$

We are given that the induced current is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. Equating the two values:

$\dfrac{\alpha}{50} = 0.88$

$\alpha = 0.88 \times 50 = 44$

Answer: $44$

subject: Physics
q: Two concentric circular coils, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, are placed in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mi>Y</mi></math> plane. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn></math>turns, and a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> has 200 turns and radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mtext>.</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> carries a time dependent current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="italic">t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is in s. The emf induced in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> at the instant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>s is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ..........
question: Two concentric circular coils, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, are placed in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mi>Y</mi></math> plane. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn></math>turns, and a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> has 200 turns and radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mtext>.</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> carries a time dependent current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="italic">t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is in s. The emf induced in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> at the instant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>s is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ..........
solution:
I=5t2+2t+C

didt=(10t+2)

ϕsmall =BA=μ0IN22Rπr2

induced emf in small coil

e=dϕdt=μ0N22rπr2N1didt=μ0N1N2πr22R(10t-2)

at t=1

e=μ0N1N2πr22R8=4μ0N1N2πr2R=4(4π)10-7×20020×500×10-410-2π

=80×π2×10-7×10×102×10-2=8×10-4 volt=0.8 mV=4x

x=5

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at right angles to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The magnitude of induced emf, between the ends of wire will be ___________.
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at right angles to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The magnitude of induced emf, between the ends of wire will be ___________.
solution:
The expression of motional emf is e=BLvsinθ, where, θ is angle between magnetic field and direction of motion.

Here, θ=90°, 

So, induced emf across the ends of wire is e= BLvsin90°=BLv

= 2 × 1 × 8 = 16 V

subject: Physics
q: A coil of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> turns and area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated at half a revolution per second and is placed in uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> perpendicular to axis of rotation of the coil. The maximum voltage generated in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math> volt. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is ______.
question: A coil of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> turns and area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated at half a revolution per second and is placed in uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> perpendicular to axis of rotation of the coil. The maximum voltage generated in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math> volt. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Time taken for one complete revolution = time period =2 s.

Therefore, ω=2πT=π rad s-1

Now flux, ϕ=NABcos(ωt)

Therefore, induced EMF ε=-dϕdt=NABωsin(ωt)

The maximum voltage generated, εmax=NABω

=200×0.2×0.01×π

=4π10=2π5 volt

Hence, β=5.

q: A rectangular loop has a sliding connector PQ of length $\ell$ and resistance $\mathrm{R} \Omega$ and it is moving with a speed $v$ as shown. The set-up is placed in a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper. The three currents $I_1, I_2$ and $I$ are
question: A rectangular loop has a sliding connector PQ of length $\ell$ and resistance $\mathrm{R} \Omega$ and it is moving with a speed $v$ as shown. The set-up is placed in a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper. The three currents $I_1, I_2$ and $I$ are
solution: A moving conductor is equivalent to a battery of emf $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{B} \ell \quad$ (motion emf) Equivalent circuit $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{l}_1+\mathrm{l}_2 $$ applying Kirchoff's law $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1 R+I R-v B \ell=0 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2 R+I R-v B \ell=0 \end{aligned} $$ adding (1) \& (2) $$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{IR}+\mathrm{IR}=2 \mathrm{vB} \ell \\ & \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \mathrm{vB} \ell}{3 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\mathrm{vB} \ell}{3 \mathrm{R}} \end{aligned} $$
